**Summary:** The lecture concludes by warning against the dangers of impersonal thinking, even in mature stages of sadhana, emphasizing the importance of surrender, gratefulness, and seeing Krishna in all circumstances, appreciating the personal form and the lessons taught through devotees.

Then you are actually heading towards an impersonal thinking and impersonal path. So you can see how dangerous this impersonal thing, impersonal route is. Even during sadhaka stage or in the mature stage, even during the mature stage, you should not think about any other person. Even on the mature stage, impersonal meditation or impersonal path is troublesome. Kleshodikata Rastesham abhyakta asakthaset. Our chetasa becomes asakti towards impersonalism. That means, I do not want to deal with all these personal persons. It is all troublesome. Better I will just mind my own thing and I will not do. If any interaction is required with other department, this and that and all that, let somebody else do. I will not do. If I am in any situation like this, I will not do. That is the common sense. This is the common sense. This is the common sense. This is the common sense. This is the common sense. This is the common sense. This is the common sense. thing and I will not do. If any interaction is required with other department this and that and all that, let somebody else do. I will not. No. This is all mentality of not wanting to surrender. Deep inside we don't want to surrender to Krishna's wish that yes this devotee is doing this particular service so I have to abide by that person. We don't want to surrender to the persons. This is the danger. We don't want to follow. We don't want to abide by the rules and regulations. Then slowly what happens? We start developing resentfulness against some other Vaishnava. This is the danger. And this Vaishnava is not willing to surrender. So offences are involved. Why we have to very carefully turn that opportunity into something very grateful. Always see Krishna as him. My Lord, you are so kind. You are personally teaching me. Appreciate personal form. Radha Govinda's personal form. My Lord, you have manifested this form for Bhuvana Mangala, for the auspiciousness of the entire universe. So obviously it is auspicious for me also. And what is that form doing? Every day teaching me. All the circumstances that I am in, every day, they are all not accidental. It is Krishna is there behind all this. And then he is, that auspicious form is bringing auspiciousness into my life on a day to day basis. But instead of taking that as auspicious, I am taking it as something, you know, not very pleasant. Then we don't want to surrender to that circumstance that has been created by God. We want to resist. And we decide better avoid all these things. Go into my own cocoon, I will not talk to anybody and not deal with anybody. This is call atrya asakti towards abhya kta, abhyaka kta sakta chetashaha. Our chetashaha will start filling up with this auspiciousness. Asakti, better avoid. So this we have to be careful. Sometimes we want, because we avoid all persons, but we need some relation. We spend more time with the cows. Because there is nobody to solve problematic dealing with human beings. Cows are good. After sometime even cows also become a troublesome situation for everybody. Because Asakti towards impersonalism increases. This is the danger. Better than that is, learn that auspicious path of the devotional service, surrender, gratefulness. My Lord, so nice you have exhibited your form. Similarly, thankful to the devotees that their personalism is teaching us, helping us in growing auspiciousness in our lives.

### brahmanas, social order, Kali Yuga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=74
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This mentality of false prestige is the cause of downfall of the perfect social order."
**Summary:** The speaker reads Srila Prabhupada's purport, explaining Shringi's justification of his father's inhospitality and comparing it to the mentality of brahmanas in Kali Yuga, highlighting the downfall of the social order due to false prestige.

Purport by Srila Prabhupada. Parikshit Cursed by a Brahmana Boy, Text 34. Translation and purport by His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupada. Certainly knew that the king asked for water from his father and the father did not respond. He tried to explain away his father's inhospitality in an impertinent manner, befitting an uncultured boy. He was not at all sorry for the king's not being well received. On the contrary, he justified the wrong act in a way characteristic of the brahmanas of Kali Yuga. He compared the king to a watchdog and so it was wrong for the king to enter the home of a brahmana and ask for water from the same pot. The dog is certainly reared by its master, but that does not mean that the dog shall claim to dine and drink from the same pot. This mentality of false prestige is the cause of downfall of the perfect social order. And we can see that, in the beginning it was started by the inexperienced son of a brahmana. As the dog is never allowed to enter within the room and hearth, although it is reared by the master, similarly, according to Shringi, the king had no right to enter the house of Shamika Rishi. The king was not allowed to enter the house of Shamika Rishi. According to the boy's opinion, the king was on the wrong side and not his father and thus, he justified his silent father. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta purport.

They are actually watchdogs. So, Shringi has the audacity to compare the king, the pure devotee of the lord, Parikshit Maharaj, who was taking care of his citizens so well. Such a personality, Shringi is comparing to a dog. grhapala nirupitaha. And then Shringi is saying, if that is the case, if this kshatriya, the king is like a grhapala, he is like a watchdog, then sakatham, how, how he can tad grhuhe dvastaha sabhandam bhoktum arhati. arhati means deserves. How such a watchdog, who is supposed to simply be at the door,

### varnashrama, brahmanas, etiquette
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=291
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "How this king dared to even think that I can drink water from the same pot as my father, Shamika Rishi, who is such a great brahmana?"
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the meaning of 'dvastaha' and Shringi's outrage at Parikshit Maharaj's presumption to drink from the same pot as Shamika Rishi, highlighting the perceived impropriety according to Vedic culture.

dvastaha, staha means to be situated, just like grhastaha means to be situated in household life. That is grhastaha, staha means to be situated. Dvastaha, dva means dwara, door. So, what is the place of the dog who is guarding the house? Can the dog come into the kitchen, into the bedroom, and into the living room? No, according to Vedic culture, no. So, today we see everywhere, all people are allowed. All kinds of creatures are allowed. But, here Shringi is telling, dvastaha, this Parikshit Maharaj, who is a grhapala, his position is dwara. He must be simply, you know, guarding the house, or the kingdom, situated outside the house, at the door. And he is saying, if that is the case, sabhandam bhoktu marhati, how this king dared to even think that I can drink water from the same pot as my father, Shamika Rishi, who is such a great brahmana, brahmanaihi, he is such a great brahmana. And this Kshatriya, this king, Parikshit, he is a grhapala, he is a watchdog. And he is saying, sabhandam bhoktum, I will drink water from the same pot as Shamika Rishi. So, this is outrageous, according to Shringi. That is the meaning of this verse.

### friendship, material vs spiritual, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=392
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "We had a wonderful theatre play, put up by the folk members. And that play involved, showing the distinction between, material friendship, which is ordinary, and spiritual friendship with Krishna, which is extraordinary."
**Summary:** The speaker transitions to a story about a play performed at a Friendship Unlimited event, contrasting the material friendship of Drona and Drupada with the spiritual friendship of Krishna and Sudama.

So, just last week, in folk, we had an event called, Friendship Unlimited. On the occasion of Friendship Day. And on that occasion, we had a wonderful theatre play, put up by the folk members. And that play involved, showing the distinction between, material friendship, which is ordinary, and spiritual friendship with Krishna, which is extraordinary. So, we had two parts in the play. The first part, talked about the friendship between, Drona and Drupada. And, we know how Drona and Drupada, promise each other, that we will help. Drupada says that, I will help you when it is needed. But Drona, when he is in need, when he comes, Drupada denies to even agree that he is my friend. He says, we are not equals, how can we be friends? And he sends him away. And that leads to, lot of many other terrible things happening, which take shape, as the story of Mahabharata proceeds. But then we have, another great friendship, described in the Bhagavatam, the friendship between, Lord Krishna and Sudama. And here also, Sudama happened to be, a poor Brahmana. But when he comes to, Krishna's palace in Dwaraka, and as soon as, Krishna hears the name of Sudama, he runs out of great affection, personally, and he goes and hugs Sudama, in front of everybody, much to the astonishment, of all the residents of Dwaraka. And without asking, Krishna gives unlimited wealth, to Sudama. So we were doing this play, and since I was involved in the play, back in the green room, I was just, before the drama, we were doing final touch up, make up and, you know, costumes of the different roles, different characters.

### brahmanas, knowledge, ISKCON
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=673
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Brahma janati, iti brahmana. He must have knowledge about the absolute truth. Then, he is a brahmana."
**Summary:** The speaker defines a true brahmana as one who has knowledge of the absolute truth, emphasizing Srila Prabhupada's vision of ISKCON as a movement for creating first-class brahmanas.

What is the qualification of a brahmana? Who is a brahmana? The Vedic scriptures tell us. Brahma janati, iti brahmana. Brahma janati, he must have knowledge about the absolute truth. Then, he is a brahmana. Merely by taking birth in a particular family, one cannot claim that he has become a qualified brahmana. This is a very important thing that Srila Prabhupada brought about many times when he was talking about the importance of brahmanas. In fact, Srila Prabhupada says that our movement, the Krishna consciousness movement, the ISKCON society, is meant for creating first class brahmanas in the society. It is meant for creating first class brahmanas. And, what is the meaning of first class brahmanas? Simply wearing a thread? No, not at all. One must actually be acquainted with the knowledge of the absolute truth. And one must also be busy in engaging activities connected with the absolute truth. Then he is a brahmana.

### varnashrama, social order, division of labor
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=751
**Verse References:** BG 4.13
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "This is merely division of labor in the society. To keep Krishna in the center, based on one's qualities, one's interest of work, one can be whatever he is."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the qualities of a brahmana, referencing the Bhagavad Gita and emphasizing that the varnashrama system is a division of labor based on qualities and interests, with Krishna at the center.

So, brahma janati iti brahmana. So there are various qualifications of a brahmana that are listed by Lord Krishna himself in the Bhagavad Gita. If you study towards the end of the Bhagavad Gita, Lord Krishna is giving what are the four different social orders. He had already mentioned that in the fourth chapter of the Gita. Lord Krishna says, So Lord Krishna says, I have divided the entire human society into four divisions. And, what is that four divisions? The brahmanas, the kshatriyas, vaishyas, and the shudras. Now is this some caste system? That is talked about in modern terms. No, this is merely division of labor in the society. To keep Krishna in the center, based on one's qualities, one's interest of work, one can be whatever he is. But, we keep Krishna in the center and work. That is called the Daivi Varanashrama system. Since this system is created by none other than the Supreme Lord himself. This is a universal thing. It's not that only in India it exists. It's a universal thing. Srila Prabhupada points out this. When he went to the America, he asked, don't you see in your society, you have intellectual people, you have administrative class of people, you have business class of people, you also have labor class of people. Everywhere you go, four classes of people exist. So this is Varanashrama system.

---
### varnashrama, brahmanas, kshatriyas, vaishyas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=875
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Intellectual people are Brahmanas, who are supposed to share knowledge to the society. They are like the brain of the body."
**Summary:** The speaker describes the roles of brahmanas, kshatriyas, and vaishyas within the varnashrama system, emphasizing their respective duties and contributions to society.

Intellectual people are Brahmanas, who are supposed to share knowledge to the society. They are like the brain of the body. In the body, they are the brain. Right? Brain is very important to give us directions. What to do, what not to do. It gives us directions, well thought decisions. Right? That is Brahmanas. And then, Kshatriyas are those who are into administration. Who are into taking care of the citizens. Protecting them. Kshatrayate iti kshatriya. This is the definition of Kshatriya. One who protects others from getting hurt. He is called a Kshatriya. Not like modern day administrative class leaders who sit in a nice bulletproof, you know, nice room, and they allow the poor soldiers to go and die on the battlefields. That is not a Kshatriya. Kshatriya must help others from getting hurt by himself exposing his body. Using weapons. That is Kshatriya. And then, Vaishyas are the business class of people. Krishi, Vaanijyam, Vaishya Karma Swabhavajam. Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita. So, what are the business, what are the, what are the activities designated for the Vaishyas? Krishi, agriculture. Goraksha, cow protection. Vaanijyam, trade and commerce. These are the things which are designated as duties for the Vaishyas. The business class of people.

### varnashrama, shudras, social harmony, brahmanas

**Content Type:** philosophy

**Key Quote:** "If everybody is doing their part in the society, then there can be peace and tranquility in the society."
**Summary:** The speaker outlines the role of shudras and emphasizes the importance of each class fulfilling its designated duty for societal harmony, then begins to list the qualities of a brahmana.

And then, Paricharyatmakam Karyam, Shudra Karma Swabhavajam. So, for the Shudras, the labor class of people, Paricharyatmakam, serving, service, physical labor, is actually the designated work. So, if everybody is doing their part in the society, then there can be peace and tranquility in the society. No one has to bother, oh now, the enemy king, country is attacking, what to do? You don't have to bother. You go on with your work. There are Kshatriyas who are well trained and qualified who will guard the country. Right? So, that is the idea in the Varanashrama system. So, what are the qualities of a Brahmana? Satyam, first of all. A Brah, to be called as a Brahmana, one must be truthful. Kshatram, Satyam. And then, Arjavam. Arjavam means, simplicity. Simple living, and high thinking, should be the philosophy of life. Not that we live in high rise buildings, but our thinking is in the Patala. It should not be like that. High thinking should be there. Simple living should be there. So, that is one of the important qualities of a Brahmana.

### brahmanas, activities, worship, study
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=1202
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A Brahmana should not think, I will worship and I will become perfect. No, he must help others, that is part of his duty, assigned service."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the activities of Yajana and Pathana, emphasizing that a brahmana must not only worship and study themselves but also help others to do the same, spreading spiritual knowledge in society.

Yajana means, worshipping the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Yajana means, helping others to engage in worshipping the Lord. A Brahmana should not think, I will worship and I will become perfect. No, he must help others, that is part of his duty, assigned service. So, Yajana, Yajana, worship the Lord, and help others to worship the Lord. And then, Pathana, Pathana, I study the Vedic scriptures, and I help others to study the Vedic scriptures, and understanding the real meaning. Pathana means to study, Pathana means to help others to study. So, a Brahmana is supposed to spread spiritual knowledge in the society, help others to become spiritually advanced.

---
### brahmanas, activities, charity, greed
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=1261
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The idea is, one accepts as much as required for him, for his purpose of serving the Lord, and the rest, he actually gives it back to the society."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the activities of Dana and Parigraha, emphasizing that a brahmana should accept only what is needed for serving the Lord and give the rest back to society, contrasting this with the stereotype of the greedy brahmana.

And then, Dana, Parigraha. Dana means to give charity, Parigraha means to accept charity. Brahmana is not simply supposed to accept charity. Whatever charity he gets from others, he engages it, only as much as it is required, he keeps it with him, and the rest he has to give it back as charity for the right cause. It's not that he just collects charity. Many times we keep hearing, Daan Dakshina is a very common word. So, whenever we see in some movies, Brahmanas are generally portrayed, you know, sometimes in a funny way, as a greedy Brahmana. He will always keep asking, Daan Dakshina Dijiye. Right? So, quality of Brahmana, work of Brahmana, is not simply to keep accepting Daan Dakshina. It's not that I'll show my sacred thread and keep accepting as much as possible. No, that is not the idea. The idea is, one accepts as much as required for him, for his purpose of serving the Lord, and the rest, he actually gives it back to the society.

---
### brahmanas, simplicity, Chanakya Pandit, Shringi
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=1334
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "A Brahmana will never actually, a real Brahmana, will never actually think that I should usurp more and more wealth."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Chanakya Pandit to illustrate the brahminical quality of simplicity and contrasts it with Shringi's behavior, setting the stage for a deeper analysis of Shringi's actions.

Srila Prabhupada gives the example of Chanakya Pandit, who was also a qualified Brahmana. And, even though he was playing a very important role in the kingdom of Chandragupta Maurya, in the Mauryan kingdom, and even though the king offered him a big palatial building for him to stay, Chanakya denied. He said, I don't want to stay in the palace. So, he preferred to stay in a humble cottage outside the palace. But he was guiding Chandragupta Maurya. He was guiding him on the principles of Vedic understanding. So, a Brahmana will never actually, a real Brahmana, will never actually think that I should usurp more and more wealth. No. Arjavam. Arjavam. Simplicity. Simple living, and high thinking. But here you see, but here you see, in this verse we see, this Brahmana boy, what are the different words used by Srila Prabhupada in the purport, to describe this Brahmana boy, Shringi. Shringi was the son of Shamika Rishi.

### Shringi, inexperience, power, curses
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/4pPYmm9wJI0?t=1410
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "When powers are given to us, when we are still inexperienced and immature, more than good, doing good to others, we end up harming others."
**Summary:** The speaker recounts the story of Parikshit Maharaj and Shringi, highlighting Shringi's inexperience and misuse of Brahmanical powers, leading to his cursing the pure devotee king.

Perhaps we all know the story, we have been discussing this verse from many weeks. Parikshit Maharaj, when he was tired, thirsty, hungry, he approaches the cottage of Shamika Rishi. Shamika Rishi was in meditation. He did not actually give a good hospitality. He did not receive the King Parikshit. So Parikshit, jokingly, he took a dead snake and put it around his neck, around the sages neck, and he went away. So when this was known to Shringi, so what did Shringi do, is what we are reading these verses. What did he think? So Prabhupada uses specific words to describe Shringi. First of all he says, inexperienced boy. You know he is an inexperienced boy. Sometimes, when powers are given to us, when we are still inexperienced and immature, more than good, doing good to others, we end up harming others. That is depicted here in Shringi. Even though he was Brahmana, he was instructed in several mantras by his father. So he was very powerful. He was following all the rules and regulations of a Brahmana. So he had that Brahmanical strength. But, since he was inexperienced, he was immature, he did not know what is the right usage of these Brahmanical powers. Therefore, he dared to curse the pure devotee, King Pariksit.

### technology, addiction, misuse of power, smartphones

**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public

**Summary:** The speaker draws a parallel between Shringi's misuse of power and the modern-day misuse of technology, particularly smartphone addiction, highlighting how it can lead to negative consequences.

Now in our situation today, if you see, all of us, we have access to the most advanced technology. Technology is the power today. Like Shringi had some powers back then, now all of us also have technology. The best technology is at the tip of our fingers. Right? We can access any content. We can, we can do anything. We can sit at our place and order food, order groceries, everything is. No doubt it has made our life very very comfortable. But, there are also the other side of this. Lot of people are becoming victims of so much of addiction to internet usage. Right? So there is power, but since we are not qualified to use the power properly, we are not trained, see, we misuse it and bring harm to ourselves. So just like Shringi had powers and he misused it to curse a pure devotee, what we are doing, this power is there, but we are not qualified to use it properly. So, lot of people becoming victims of the smartphone addiction. Smartphone is supposed to make us smart, isn't it? But it is not making us smart, it is actually making us dull, dumb. It's a fact. Of course, it's not that we should not use smartphone. We should use as much as it is required. Regulated usage will give us real benefit. But if we use it more than required, every now and then seeing the smartphone, every now and then want to check what is the notification, every now and then...

So chanting is going on on one hand, chatting is going on on other hand. Chatting and chanting simultaneously going on. Krishna is confused. Is he talking to me or is he talking to somebody? Chanting means we are talking to Krishna. It is our private time with Krishna, with the holy name. We are supposed to spend quality time with Krishna. But instead of doing that, we are interrupting in between. Every day we chant the ten offences to be avoided against chanting the holy names of the Lord. And the last offence, it is also offensive to be inattentive while chanting. That's an offence. It is listed in the Padma Purana. Ten offences against chanting. So if we want to make quick progress towards the desired goal of chanting, which is to attain pure love for Krishna, we must stay away from these offences. Right? ## The Power and Importance of Deity Worship I Sri Satya Gaura Chandra Dasa I 3.9.17 I 08.06.2023
**Speaker:** HG Satya Gaura Chandra Dasa

### devotional service, archana, material attachment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ipc_u8jgO8k?t=52
**Verse References:** BG 2.47
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "There is no auspicious activity except archana of the Lord."
**Summary:** This segment discusses the importance of devotional service (archana) as instructed in the Narada Pancharatra, contrasting it with the fruitless endeavors of those attached to material pursuits and rebellious activities like jnana and yoga when not aimed at Vishnu.

People in general all engage in foolish acts, not in the really beneficial activities enunciated directly by you for their guidance. As long as their tendency for foolish work remains powerful, all their plans in the struggle for existence will be cut to pieces. I therefore offer my obeisances unto him who acts as eternal time. People in general are all engaged in senseless work. They are systematically unmindful of the real beneficial work, which is the devotional service of the Lord, technically called the Archana regulations. The Archana regulations are directly instructed by the Lord in the Narada Pancharatra and are strictly followed by the intelligent men who know well that the highest perfectional goal of life is to reach Lord Vishnu, who is the root of the tree. Because of their foolish activities, they are unaware that the ultimate goal of human life is to achieve Vishnu, the lord of the cosmic manifestation, and so their struggle for existence is in the wrong direction of material civilization, which is under the external energy. They are led by similar foolish persons, just as one blind man is led by another blind man, and both fall in the ditch. Such foolish men cannot be attracted towards the activities of the supreme powerful, who is actually the neutralizing measure for their foolish activities, unless and until they have the good sense to be guided by the great souls, who are completely freed from material attachment. In Bhagavad Gita, the Lord asks everyone to give up all other occupational duties and absolutely engage in archana activities or in pleasing the Lord, but almost no one is attracted to such archana activity. Everyone is more or less attracted by activities which are conditions of rebellion against the supreme Lord. The systems of jnana and yoga are also indirectly rebellious acts against the Lord. There is no auspicious activity except archana of the Lord. Jnana and yoga are sometimes accepted within the purview of archana when the ultimate aim is Vishnu and not otherwise. The conclusion is that only the devotees of the Lord are bona fide human beings eligible for salvation. Others are vainly struggling for existence without any actual benefit. Thus ends the book.

The book is about a very big cruise ship, that means a ship for pleasure. So they called it Global Dream 2. Their goal is to build the largest ship in the world, meant for pleasure. And then it appears that they have spent 11,000 crores to build this ship. It has 20 floors. It is done by one company called MV Werftan, it's a German Hong Kong company. They have mentioned the dimensions also, some 342 meters and 2 lakh ton weight and it has an outdoor water park in the ship only. And there are many cinema halls, you know, they made it really nice, 11,000 crores, and then of course they took a lot of years, it's a Global Dream 2. And then what happened recently? This company, MV Werftan, whatever, the building, building this ship, they went bankrupt, that means money is, they are out of money. And so they are trying to sell this ship, the Global Dream. And nobody is ready to purchase. Actually it can accommodate around 9,000 passengers. There must be a big ship, 20 floors and a very, very big ship. So nobody is coming forward. Meanwhile, where the ship is already, so to say, tied or parked, that port was stolen by these people.

That shloka defines what is devotional service. When, when London left India, some 75 years back, India was in a very poor condition, they were exploited, India was exploited so much, they become very poor, so some people are commenting that now see how India has taken over. Both of them are competing for puna puna sharvita sharvanana, in that somebody has become fifth, somebody has become sixth, it doesn't hardly matters, which kind, but then the point is money is there, that money has to be taken away from them and engaged in archana. ### fruitive activities, purification, surrender
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ipc_u8jgO8k?t=1389

**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of milk and yogurt to explain how to cure the disease of fruitive activities, advocating for taking a portion of people's hard-earned money and engaging it in archana to purify their hearts and encourage surrender to Krishna.

Prabhupada explains if somebody eats lot of milk sweets, you will have indigestion and motion loss, etc. and all those things you need one more milk product called curds or yogurt to cure the disease caused by milk. Now milk and yogurt are one, but an expert physician knows how to use which milk product to use to cure the disease. Narada Pancharatra is about that. People are suffering because of the fruitive activity, they are suffering because they don't know how to utilize this money. Now here comes Narada Pancharatra, offer your results to Krishna. Bhagavad Gita also says the same thing, karma yoga means okay, you are working hard, you are earning a lot of money, a lot of fruitive activity, fine, offer your results to Krishna. This is only solution if you want to help these fruitive workers who are bound by tepeesha tantra urudhamni baddha, you can do it by doing this. Who are bound by all the, you know, the tight rope of this fruitive activity, the only mercy that you can show is take away a portion of their hard earned money. If they give money, you will see what a purification comes in their hearts. If you want to bring the best purification, take away their money and engage in Archana. They'll feel so nice. Next time when they came they're very light. So what happened? I'm thinking how to save this money and keep it under sofa, so many plans Prabhu. Now it's all gone. I'm very happy, I'm very light actually. Please understand, people are suffering because they don't.

He told a proverb, one man asked another, two persons are there, one man asked another, are you intelligent? The second man started to look at his pockets, he started touching his pockets, he started touching his pockets, he started touching his pockets, he started touching his pockets. The first man asked, why are you looking into your pockets? The second man said, well, if there is any money there in the pocket, that means I am intelligent. So Prabhupada explained, actually in our movement everything is going on by my intelligence, is Prabhupada's intelligence and your cooperation. So you are preaching very intelligently, but if there is not money, then where is your intelligence? Prabhupada intelligence may smash kar diya preaching ho gaya. ### shelter, devotee, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/Ipc_u8jgO8k?t=2137
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers

### material nature, supreme control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rdRF7G7EG7g?t=367
**Verse References:** BG 9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "It is not that nature works blindly."
**Summary:** The speaker begins the purport, explaining that material energy is impregnated by the glance of the Supreme Lord and that nature works under His direction and will, not blindly. He emphasizes the difference between personalists and impersonalists, stating that the Lord is never bewildered.

And from that Mahatattva the material manifestation has emerged. In the Vedic literatures it is said that by the glance of the Supreme Lord, the total material energy is impregnated and then everything is born of the material nature. It is also confirmed in the 9th chapter of Bhagavad Gita that under His glance, Adhyakshena, under His direction and by His will, nature is working. It is not that nature works blindly. After understanding the position of the conditioned souls in relation to material nature, Devahuti wanted to know how nature works under the direction of the Lord and what the relationship is between the material nature and the Lord. In other words, she wanted to learn the characteristics of the Supreme Lord in relation to the material nature. The relationship of the living entities with matter and that of the Supreme Lord is not the same. The Supreme Lord with matter are certainly not on the same level, although the Mayavadis may interpret it in that way. When it is said that the living entities are bewildered, the Mayavadi philosophers ascribe this bewilderment to the Supreme Lord. But that is not applicable. The Lord is never bewildered. That is the difference between personalists and impersonalists. Devahuti is not unintelligent. She has enough intelligence to understand that the Supreme Lord is not bewildered. She has enough intelligence to understand that the Supreme Lord is not bewildered. That means that the living entities are not on the same level of the Supreme Lord. That means that the Supreme Lord is not unintelligent. That is the difference between personalists and impersonalists. Because the living entities are inf

### material nature, supreme control
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/rdRF7G7EG7g?t=462
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Material nature is ever existing in its subtle form as the energy of the lord but it sometimes manifests this non-existent temporally existent nature, the cosmos."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the purport, explaining that the Lord is not in contact with nature and is the supreme controller. He clarifies the meaning of 'Sat' and 'Asat' in the verse, stating that the cosmic manifestation is Asat (temporary) while the material energy of the Lord is Sat (ever-existing).

overlap. That means that they are not in contact with nature. by spiritual nature nor by material nature. He is the supreme controller himself and he cannot be compared to the ordinary living entities who are controlled by the laws of material nature. Two words used in this verse are Sat and Asat. The cosmic manifestation is Asat, it does not exist but the material energy of the supreme lord is Sat or ever existing. Material nature is ever existing in its subtle form as the energy of the lord but it sometimes manifests this non-existent temporally existent nature, the cosmos. An analogy may be made with the father and mother. The mother and father exist but sometimes the mother begets children. Similarly, this cosmic manifestation which comes from the unmanifest material nature of the supreme lord sometimes appears and again disappears but the material nature is ever exist. And the lord is the supreme cause for both the subtle and gross manifestations of this material world.

And they are not visible to us. That is unmanifest world. Unmanifest creation.

Mahatatva is present in each one of our bodies. From Pramahatvatva comes the whole manifestation of this material world and Mahatvatva also enters into each and every body. ### manifestation, unmanifest

So, this is Mahatvatva. And as you see in the creation, first is unmanifest creation and then comes manifest creation. So, say for example you see somebody behaving in a certain way. What you are seeing is manifest creation. But behind this manifest creation there is an unmanifest creation. What he thinks in his mind, what is he creating in the Chittam, that is called unmanifest creation. Which we will not be able to see. But for anything to appear manifest, there has to be unmanifest work also.

When Newton saw he saw a red apple falling not the green. Green apples won't fall. So obviously although law of gravitation is there but why the green leaves are not falling? Why the green leaves or green apples or green fruits why they are not falling? Gravitation law is there but you see big the clouds are carrying tons of water they are not falling. So why is this law of gravitation so why is this law of gravitation?

## What Is the Difference Between Being Educated and Wise | Sri Sampati Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Satya Gaura Chandra Dasa

### education, wisdom, lust
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/s6DnRRV6lgo?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Difference between being educated [and] being wise."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the topic of the difference between education and wisdom using Ravana's lust for Sita as an example of being educated but not wise.

How to educate people, how to earn property in the rightful manner. Ravana was also called brahmin. He was educated. What did he do when he heard about the beauty of Mother Sita? He became lusty. [This is the] difference between being educated [and] being wise.

---
### education, wisdom, arrogance, greed
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/s6DnRRV6lgo?t=13
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Even though he was a pandit, he was son of a brahmana sage Vishravas, he was not a wise person; he had so much of arrogance, he was lusty, he was greedy."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on Ravana's high education and lineage, emphasizing that despite his qualifications, he lacked wisdom due to his arrogance, lust, and greed.

Ramayana teaches an important lesson for all of us, especially in the modern day: the difference between being educated [and] being wise. We find the example of Ravana, who was highly educated, who was called pandit. Ravana was called pandit. He is a son of a great sage called Vishravas, a brahmana sage Vishravas, and Ravana was also called brahmin. Right, people say that he is brahmin. The brahmin is the one who knows the Vedas, who has got a lot of knowledge. But unfortunately, even though he was a pandit, he was son of a brahmana sage Vishravas, he was not a wise person; he had so much of arrogance, he was lusty, he was greedy.

---
### lust, greed, education, wrongful action
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/s6DnRRV6lgo?t=77
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "His education didn't teach him how to be satisfied; his education didn't teach him how to earn property in the rightful manner."
**Summary:** The speaker details Ravana's actions of kidnapping Sita and stealing Lanka from Kubera, highlighting how his education failed to instill satisfaction or ethical behavior.

When he heard about the beauty of Mother Sita, he became lusty, and he went to kidnap Mother Sita, who was married, was happily living with her husband Shri Ram, but he went and kidnapped her. His education didn't help him, and he was educated. What did he do? He went and snatched Lanka from his half-brother Kubera. So he was power-hungry, he wanted property of Lanka, and he was hungry. So he was not satisfied. His education didn't teach him how to be satisfied; his education didn't teach him how to earn property in the rightful manner. In a wrong way, he snatched property from his own brother Kubera. He went and kidnapped a married woman because he was lusty.

---
### wisdom, detachment, satisfaction, education
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/s6DnRRV6lgo?t=123
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ram was very satisfied with what was there in his life; he was not an ambitious, over-ambitious person like Ravana."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Ravana with Lord Rama, who, despite being educated, demonstrated wisdom by choosing the forest over Ayodhya and being content with his circumstances.

But he was highly educated. On the other hand, we see the great personality Lord Shri Ram, who was also educated, but more than educated, he was a wise person. The moment he was asked to choose between Ayodhya and going to [the] forest, he happily chose to go to [the] forest and gave up Ayodhya to his own brother Bharat. In fact, in Ramayana, we see when Bharat was ready to offer Ayodhya back to Ram, Ram was very happy being in the forest. He wanted Bharat to continue to be the king and take care of Ayodhya till he returns. So Ram was very satisfied with what was there in his life; he was not an ambitious, over-ambitious person like Ravana who was ready to snatch property from his own brother.

---
### self-control, wisdom, promise, lust
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/s6DnRRV6lgo?t=175
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Ram exhibited self-control."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to contrast Rama and Ravana, highlighting Rama's self-control and adherence to his vow of fidelity, even when tempted by Shurpanakha.

Here Ram is happily handing over Ayodhya to his own brother Bharat. And Ram, even though Shurpanakha came and offered herself, Ram did not become lusty. He had control over himself. He used his wisdom, he used his power, his knowledge to have control over himself, to follow the promise given to his own wife, that ekapatni vratasta. When a woman, a beautiful woman, coming and offering herself with her own consent, 'Let's have fun,' Ram said, 'No, I cannot. I would like to follow my promise, and I'm not interested in this.' But Ravana became lusty. Ram exhibited self-control.

---
### character, education, wisdom, modern society
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/s6DnRRV6lgo?t=218
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Ramayana teaches us mere education is not enough; we have to make people wise."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by emphasizing the need for wisdom and character development alongside education, using the example of educated terrorists to illustrate the dangers of lacking character.

So we need wise people, not just knowledge of the world, but knowledge of the world and knowledge of the people, not educated people. In today's society, we are all talking about how to educate people, but Ramayana teaches us mere education is not enough; we have to make people wise, and they become wise when they develop character like Lord Shri Ram, not like Ravana. If you are only concerned about education, not character building, then we will have a lot of educated, characterless people like Ravana. Like we hear many times when the police arrest some of the terrorists, some of the terrorists happen to be highly educated, like double degree, like medical, they have done their MBBS, they have done their masters in some engineering field, and they have become terrorists. So we need people like Lord Shri Ram, and Shri Ram teaches us the importance of wisdom, importance of knowledge, importance of character along with education. That is the lesson we learn from Shri Valmiki Ramayana.

## What lies behind the happiness facade? | HG Satya Gaura Chandra Dasa | SB 1.13.2 | 19-01-2020
**Speaker:** HG Satya Gaura Chandra Dasa

### Srimad Bhagavatam, Vidura, Maitreya Muni
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Vidura retired from putting questions before Maitreya Muni when he was convinced by Maitreya Rishi that the Sanmam Bonam of life is to be finally situated in the transcendental loving service of Lord Shri Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker reads from Srimad Bhagavatam, explaining how Vidura, after questioning Maitreya Muni, became convinced that the ultimate goal of life is loving service to Lord Krishna, thus ending his inquiries.

Reading from Srimad Bhagavatam, Canto 1, Chapter 13, and Text 2: Yavatah Krutavan Prashnan Chattakhaushara Vagratah, Jatayika Bhaktir Govinde Tebhyas Chopra Ramah. Translation and purport by His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Shri Prabhupada. Translation: After asking various questions and becoming established in the transcendental loving service of Lord Krishna, Vidura retired from putting questions to Maitreya Muni. Purport by A.S. Prabhupada: Vidura retired from putting questions before Maitreya Muni when he was convinced by Maitreya Rishi that the Sanmam Bonam of life is to be finally situated in the transcendental loving service of Lord Shri Krishna, who is Govinda or one who satisfies His devotees in all respects. The conditioned soul, the living being in material existence, seeks happiness by employing his senses in the modes of materialism, but that cannot give him satisfaction. He then searches after the supreme truth by the empiric-philosophic speculative method and intellectual feats. But if he doesn't find the ultimate goal, he again goes down to material activities and engages himself in various philanthropic and altruistic works, which all fail to give him satisfaction. So, neither fructive activities nor dry philosophical speculation can give one satisfaction, because by nature, a living being is the eternal servitor of the Supreme Lord Shri Krishna. And all the Vedic literatures give him direction towards that ultimate end. The Bhagavad Gita confirms this statement. Like Vidura, an inquisitive conditioned soul must approach a bona fide spiritual master like Maitreya and by intelligent inquiries must try to know everything about karma, fruitive activities, jnana, philosophical research for the supreme truth, and yoga, the linking process of spiritual realization. One who is not seriously inclined to put questions before a spiritual master need not accommodate a show bottle spiritual master, nor should a person who may be a spiritual master for others pose to be so if he is unable to engage his disciple, ultimately in the transcendental loving service of Lord Sri Krishna. Vidura was successful in approaching such a spiritual Master like Maitreya and he got the ultimate goal of life, Bhakti unto Govinda. Thus, there was nothing to be known further about spiritual progress. Thus ends the Bhaktivedanta Bhagavata.

---
### Questions, Happiness, Devotional Service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=188
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Actually we don't have any retirement for from questions and seeking answers as soon as we get up in the morning we start off our questions and answers and then it goes on till we take rest."
**Summary:** The speaker introduces the context of the lecture, focusing on the conversation between Vidura and Maitreya Rishi, and highlights the importance of questions and answers in life, emphasizing that true satisfaction comes from devotional service to Krishna.

We are reading from the chapter, Dhritarashtra quits home. And here, till this verse, the conversation between a great devotee by name Vidura and Maitreya Rishi was described. And in the last verse, Atmano Gathim, the destination for the Self or the Soul was discussed. And here, it is said that Yavatah Krutavan Prashnan, Vidura asked all these questions. And uh jatayika bhakti govinde. When he understood that Atmana Gathi is ekabhakti, ekabhakti govinde, the unalloyed devotional service to Lord Shri Krishna. Then Tebhyescha Upararama, he has retired from asking questions. Actually we don't have any retirement for from questions and seeking answers as soon as we get up in the morning we start off our questions and answers and then it goes on till we take rest some people may dream also about answers and questions and answers how can i become a millionaire in one month that's the question and dream will answer you know like that people are continuously asking questions and trying to get answers and so those questions and answers as long as they do not reach ekabhaktir govinde if they do not reach the govinde, they do not reach to the point of loving devotional service to Krishna then such questions will continue now why we ask questions everybody wants to be happy yes.

### Happiness, India, Satisfaction
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=327
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Externally, people can look like they are happy but when it comes to satisfaction, that is a different kind of a situation."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the pursuit of happiness, referencing the United Nations' happiness report and a survey conducted in India, highlighting the contrast between outward appearances of happiness and true inner satisfaction.

Anybody wants to be unhappy anybody goes to the temple for give me unhappiness they all go in order to ask the lord to remove all the unhappiness right recently recently means a couple of years back the united nations has released a report it's called happiness report of the world you know they go to many individuals they ask certain questions and they try to assess the happiness of a country so united nations has published an article about the happiness this thing and then india was given 118th position first means the most happiest and around 125 or 130 is the lowest in happiness so we got 118th that is the happiness rank probably they have not come to our temple those so those guys were trying to do survey so so then what happened some six people they wondered how india can be 118th so they decided to do a survey by themselves they said okay we'll go all over the country and figure out what makes indians happy that is their journey they travelled 10,000 kilometers by their vehicle travelled all parts of the country met hundreds and thousands of people and uh during all these things they went through a lot of turmoil sometimes you know car breakdown in the middle of a forest and then somebody will come and help them .. you know they were., right..? writing their experiences of traveling 10,000 kilometers. And in India, you know, some of the roads, you know, any car, any car will get spoiled. It is, you know, some of the roads are like that. So, they traveled such far distances and to figure out this. In Chhattisgarh, I think they met one person and they asked, tell me, you know, about your happiness. What makes you happy? Then that person said, whenever you find happiness, you let me know. I'm still searching for it. You know, that is one of the answers he gave. And one person, he doesn't have an eye, but still he looked very happy. Somebody has, you know, a very small setup, hardly any house, but still he is, you know, happy. After doing all these things, these people wondered how India, you know, is faring 118th position. Looks like everybody is happy. A person who doesn't have a house, a person who has only one eye, a person who is still seeking happiness, everybody looks okay.

### Happiness, Satisfaction, Materialism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=539
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Externally, people can look like they are happy but when it comes to satisfaction, that is a different kind of a situation."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the difference between external happiness and internal satisfaction, using examples of celebrities who, despite their success, suffer from loneliness, anxiety, and depression, illustrating the emptiness of material pursuits.

So, they concluded their article by saying that, you know, India is a happy country. Actually, there is nothing lacking in our people. So, the person, here in the purport, if you see, Prabhupada is saying that everybody is seeking happiness. And you ask anybody, how are you? Instantly, what will be the answer? He'll say, I'm fine. He may be in ICU, one leg is hanging, you know, full bandage, whole body. And then you go and ask him, how are you? I'm fine. Little problem. It's not little, he's in ICU. So, that's the instinctive, you know, response. But Prabhupada here is talking about one more deeper aspect. He's talking about satisfaction. Externally, people can look like they are happy but when it comes to satisfaction, that is a different kind of a situation. So, Prabhupada is saying, although everybody is trying to enjoy in the materialism, but nobody is happy, nobody is satisfied. Without satisfaction, happiness is not possible. Satisfaction is required. So, they did one survey of the big names of the world. Like, of course, in our country, say Deepika Padukone, and then Prince Harris, Prince Harry London, Michael Phelps, you know, he is a swimmer. Like that, they have asked them certain questions. So, Deepika Padukone, she said, I used to get angry. Anxiety attacks. And then I used to always feel hollow from within. And then they asked Prince Harry, he said, 20 years, I was in depression. And then Michael Phelps, he said, he said, I always used to feel loneliness. Loneliness, always. And then I am grateful that I have not committed suicide, but then I was so lonely. And then I am grateful that I have not committed suicide, but then I was so lonely that all these thoughts were crossing my mind. Now, if you see all these people, either in the movies or TVs and newspapers, you will see their, you know, big smiles and bright, colorful faces and energetic moves and, you know, interesting movements and all those things we will see. And then, you know, you know, we think that they are, you know, yes, they must be happy because everything is there for them. They are having it all, whereas I don't have. And then, so, since they are having it all, and since I don't have, if I can also have it all, if I also have everything with me, then probably I will be happy. So, this happiness and satisfaction is a big subject in the outside world.

### Krishna Consciousness, Dukkha, Happiness Façade
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=780
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Similarly, many of these stars and heroes and all that, they put a happiness façade. But what is there inside? We don't know, whether it is a shed or a concrete building or what, we don't know. That is the situation."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the superficiality of happiness in the material world, comparing it to a façade, and explains how even those who seem to have everything may be suffering internally, referencing the concept of Dukkhalayam from the Bhagavad Gita.

For centuries, they are discussing, yes, what is this? What makes a person happy, satisfied and happy? Sometimes, once, when we come to Krishna Consciousness, in the beginning days, like, sometimes, one person asked me, Swamiji, Bhagavad Gita says that it is Dukkhalayam. This world is full of Dukkha. But then, when I went to MG road, where is the Dukkha? Everybody is colourful. All buildings are vibrating. So, where is this Dukkha? You know, this is, this is what. So, similarly, these stars are also, they are part of the whole, it's called happiness façade. Façade means, you know, when you see a nice building, front there will be façade. In Hyderabad, you can see, outside façade will be very interesting. But when you see behind, it is actually a shed. Front, it is very nice, you know, very good façade. So, similarly, many of these stars and heroes and all that, they put a happiness façade. But what is there inside? We don't know, whether it is a shed or a concrete building or what, we don't know. That is the situation.

### Social Pressure, Personality, Suffering
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=877
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So they move away from their real personality more and more in order to put up a happiness facade, some other personality outside and so they suffer inside."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that people maintain a 'happiness façade' due to social pressure and competition, leading them to suppress their true selves and experience inner suffering, loneliness, and emptiness.

Now, why do people do this? Because, because of the competition, social pressure. There will be social pressure. Let us say, you lived in a, you are using a Ford car. And you are living in a big bungalow. And let us say, situation has become worse. Now, you have to live in a Maruti 800. Not easy. Why? Because we all know, there is a certain social pressure. So, the stars and everybody also, they will be under tremendous pressure to maintain their happiness façade. They maintain their levels, to maintain their facilities. They will all be very conscious of all these things. It is a natural thing in the outside world, in the society. So, when they are like that, trying to be somebody. But there is no way to be somebody. You cannot be like this. You can't be yourself. You cannot be yourself. You cannot be yourself. You cannot be about yourself. You cannot be somebody. And actually it is not that. This is what we see. What are these people trying to do? They want to put on some personality. But actually inside that's not their real personality. So they move away from their real personality more and more in order to put up a happiness facade, some other personality outside and so they suffer inside. They start feeling lonely, empty, hollow. All these issues will be there. But even if you think inside personality, if it is not understood and is only maintaining an outside stuff, he will not be happy. A similar situation happens with all the living entities in this world. By nature, we are all something inside. But we are all trying to be putting up a different personality outside.

---
### Real Personality, Humility, Sanatana Goswami
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=1024
**Verse References:** BG 15
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "All these top heroes. See the problem with them is they will not be able to come out of it and ask somebody, I need help."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the real personality as explained in the Bhagavad Gita and highlights the difficulty of those maintaining a façade to admit their weaknesses, drawing a parallel to Sanatana Goswami and Rupa Goswami before meeting Chaitanya Mahaprabhu.

And what is our real personality? That is explained in the Bhagavad Gita, 15th chapter: mamayi vamso jivaloke, jivaloke, bhootha sanatana. All these top heroes. See the problem with them is they will not be able to come out of it and ask somebody, I need help. There is one more issue. When you are trying to put up the whole thing, you don't like to disclose your weaknesses. You don't like to disclose your miseries, your sorrows. You don't want to. Why? Because that is against what people are perceiving about your personality. So, you know, these people are caught up in a kind of a thing and then they cannot come out of it. They cannot even disclose. A similar situation was there for Sanatana Goswami and Rupa Goswami before they came in touch with Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. They were big ministers in the court of the king, that time king, a Muslim king. And so, they were actually very big ministers. They have all the facilities of that age and having it all. They are also having it all. Everything is there. But then they understood that we are actually not happy. And when Sanatana Goswami approached Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, he said, It is actually a Bengali, nice verse. So, Sanatana Goswami is asking this question to Chaitanya Mahaprabhu. This is very important. It requires humility. To be able to submit and tell, Yes, I am feeling like this. I have this trouble. I am like this. What do you think is the solution? So, they went and asked.

---
### Sanatana Goswami, Intelligent Question, Miseries
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=1156
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Many times we think if I have a lot of money, I can cross over all my miseries, all my unhappiness, my ill health, my this, that, everything I can cross over."
**Summary:** The speaker continues the story of Sanatana Goswami, emphasizing his intelligent question to Chaitanya Mahaprabhu about his true identity and the cause of his suffering, despite having material possessions and recognition.

Who am I? What is my internal personality? I am trying to put up a personality like a big minister and a happy man and having it all and all those things. But, Why am I suffering like this? Tapatraya means there are three kinds of miseries every living entity goes through. Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama, Hare Rama, Rama Rama, Hare Hare. So, Sanatana Goswami is asking this intelligent question. Saying, Yeah, everybody calls me Pandit. Everybody calls me, Oh, you are a great man. You are a great minister and I am having it all but why am I suffering? If I am so powerful, if I am having it all, why am I unable to resolve my miseries? Many times we think if I have a lot of money, I can cross over all my miseries, all my unhappiness, my ill health, my this, that, everything I can cross over. The moment I have everything with me, but then Sanatana Goswami is saying, Yes, I am having everything with me, but nonetheless, I am unable to solve my issues. So, he is asking, Who am I? You know, I am trying to put up something that is fine, but I am not happy. Inside I am feeling hollow, depressed, all these things. You know, tapatraya, all three kinds of miseries are haunting me. But then, who am I? Iha nahi janiya, I do not know. Khe mane hitahai. What is the real welfare, real benefit of me? I don't know. Actually, this question is considered to be a very intelligent question.

---
### Intelligent Question, Stock Value, Identity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=1275
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Otherwise, we may be putting up one show and going on in our life. We may not even know what is our innermost desires."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the intelligent question of 'Who am I?' with mundane questions about cricket scores and stock values, emphasizing that the former leads to true satisfaction while the latter can cause anxiety and even health problems.

As I said, we all will be asking a lot of questions in our life. For example, throughout the day, if there is a, say, cricket match, India and Pakistan, cricket match, let us say, somehow you are away from the TV, generally we will be glued to the TV, but let us say you are away from that. What will you do? Continuously, you are checking, what is the score now? What is the score now? Now, this is the question. What is the score now? Is the question. That question's answer will either increase your blood pressure, or it will decrease a little bit. It depends on that answer. Okay, so, similarly, some of us will be asking questions. What is the today's stock value? We have invested in some stocks. What's happening today? As soon as America killed Iran's second man, the topmost second man, the Indian stocks fell hugely. Some 2 lakh crore or 3 lakh crore worth of loss on that day. Just the previous day, the stocks have gone up to the peak. On that day, suddenly, something happened and everything fell down. So, sometimes I see, I hear people looking at this stock thing, sometimes they get heart attacks by looking at those. So, everybody is asking questions and seeking answers. But what is the most intelligent question? This is the most intelligent question. Who am I? Is the most intelligent question.

---
### Satisfaction, Innermost Desires, Eternal Servant
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=1393
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "If you don't know who you are, you are trying to satisfy, but then it's not possible like that. You don't even know who you are."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding one's true identity and innermost desires for achieving real satisfaction, and explains that Chaitanya Mahaprabhu revealed to Sanatana Goswami that he is an eternal servant of Krishna.

What can really satisfy me within my core? This is the real intelligent question. Otherwise, we may be putting up one show and going on in our life. We may not even know what is our innermost desires. We may not even know our innermost personality. We will just go on in our life putting up something and then trying to maintain that position throughout our life. That will be our struggle. So, when as soon as Sanatana Goswami asked this, yes, who am I? So, immediately, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu replied that Jeevera Swaroopai Nithya Krishna Das. He says, deep inside, you are actually an eternal servant of Krishna. Eternal servant of Krishna. So, as soon as we understand our identity, then we can work for our real benefit. We can work for the real welfare. We can work for real happiness and satisfaction. If you don't know who you are, you are trying to satisfy, but then it's not possible like that. You don't even know who you are.

**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Unless and until he realizes who is he, he realizes that I am the son of an emperor or a king he will not be having that satisfaction."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a king's lost son raised in the forest to illustrate that true happiness and satisfaction can only be found when one realizes their true identity and acts accordingly.

Sometimes, we hear in some movies, you know, when a king goes to the forest along with his small child and somehow something happens in the forest. The king goes back to the kingdom. The child is lost in the forest. Throws and becomes like, you know, some kind of a tarjan or whatever, you know, you say, you know, becomes like that. Now, I mean, just for the sake of some argument, that boy can be happy only in the kingdom. But now he is in a forest. He will not be happy. Unless and until he realizes who is he, he realizes that I am the son of an emperor or a king he will not be having that satisfaction.

### Identity, Lion Cub, Grass
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=1539
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "As the lion is growing, it is thinking, I am also a deer."
**Summary:** The speaker shares the story of a lion cub raised among deer, highlighting how the cub, unaware of its true identity, tries to find satisfaction in eating grass like the deer, emphasizing the importance of realizing one's true nature.

One more example. Once you know, one deer was there and then one lion attacked the deer. It's a pregnant deer and then it has conceived the child, the cub, the deer cub and the mother died. And so that cub grew up among the lions. You know, that's the story. The lions will not leave the cub like that. But anyway, the deer cub has grown into a mature or a bigger animal. All the while, sorry, the other way around, there are many lions and one, lion has given birth to a cub lion. And the cub lion grew among deers. As the lion is growing, it is thinking, I am also a deer. It is continuously thinking, I am also a deer only. And like the deer, it is also eating grass. It is also eating some vegetables and it is also moving here and there. Everything is almost like a deer. Once one more big lion came and it is pouncing on all the deers. Everybody is running here and there. And this small lion deer also, it is also running here and there. And finally the big one caught this small one. And the small one is saying, leave me, leave me. Big one is saying, why are you fearful? No, I am a deer. Come on, wake up. You are not a deer. You are actually a lion. So, that child lion has a big revelation. Wow, really? So, you are not going to eat me? No. So, when the lion is there, but like a deer, it is eating grass because it doesn't know who is he.

### Spirit Soul, Devotional Service, Unalloyed
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=1691
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What satisfies the spirit soul is unalloyed devotional service to Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that just as the lion cub cannot find satisfaction in eating grass, we, as spirit souls, cannot be satisfied by material pursuits, and that true satisfaction comes from unalloyed devotional service to Krishna.

It is trying to take satisfaction in grass, vegetables. Lion's food is different. Internally it is a lion only. Unless it eats that, it cannot have satisfaction. So, similarly we are a spirit soul. You know, there is a display there. Behind you can see. We are a spirit soul inside. As long as we don't know what is spirit soul, what satisfies the spirit soul, you may be doing many other things. Like eating like this lion cub, it is eating grass and many other things to satisfy itself. But it can never be because it is a lion. Similarly, we are behaving, we are taking many, many things. We are trying to satisfy this body. But it will not satisfy us because we are actually a spirit soul inside. So, what is the satisfaction for the spirit soul? Association of the Supreme Personality. What satisfies the spirit soul is unalloyed devotional service to Krishna. You know this metallurgy. Somebody from engineering background, you know. Metallurgy is an alloy. Alloy means, you know, you mix some 2-3 metals and come out with one alloy. It is called. Unalloyed means there is no mix of any other thing. It is pure. Unalloyed. So, what is expected is unalloyed devotional service to Govinda Krishna. That means our devotional service cannot be mixed with any other thing. So, this is also called pure devotional service. This is one important point that we have to understand. Bhagavatam is not presenting Bhakti. It is presenting Ek Bhakti Govinda. It is presenting unalloyed exclusive devotional service to Krishna. It is presenting pure unalloyed devotional service to Krishna alone. Right?

---
### Ek Bhakti Govinda, Motivation, Sense Gratification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=1813
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "To love God for what he is a wonderful beautiful powerful merciful, magnanimous personality for what he is just to love him for what he is that is a that is an extraordinary devotion."
**Summary:** The speaker distinguishes between general bhakti and Ek Bhakti Govinda, explaining that the latter involves pure, unmotivated devotional service to Krishna, without seeking any personal gain or sense gratification.

Otherwise, Bhakti we will see so much. You know, in this world there is so much Bhakti is there. But world is not getting any better. Why? Because what is missing is Ek Bhakti Govinda. Now, what is this meaning? When we do devotional service, if you have an intention that I should get something out of it, that is called mixed Bhakti. Alloyed, not unalloyed. For example, we go to any temple or Tripathi, you know, you take a big list of what to ask God when I go. Right? We are busy reading it out. No, I want this. Meanwhile, they will kick you out. You are out of the temple. You have not finished your... So that Bhakti is okay. It is good that you are coming to the Supreme Lord. But that is not sufficient. Your questions will not stop. After asking God also, you will be anxious. Whether it will come or not. Will Balaji give me? Will Govinda give me? Or not? One more anxiety. No. Bhagavatam is not talking about that Bhakti. It is talking about Jataika Ek Bhaktir Govinda. Exclusive, unalloyed devotional service to Krishna. That means, while rendering service to Krishna, we have no motivation. We are not going to ask anything from the Lord. No motivation. Ahaituki apratihata. No Hetu. Why are you chanting? Why are you coming to the temple? Why are you dancing? Why are you honoring Prasad? No Hetu. An attraction towards the Lord. That's all. No other mix for our own pleasure. We don't look for our sense gratification. We only want to always see Krishna happy.

---
### Exclusive Devotion, Table Devotion, Unceasing Devotion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/ZyiAShpZT7k?t=1971
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "As soon as we get something, cloud. So that is the issue."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts the concept of exclusive devotion to Krishna with common religious practices that focus on seeking material benefits, and emphasizes that true devotion is unceasing and independent of circumstances.

This is something revolutionary for our Indians. Or in fact any other religion you take. Everybody talks about, this prayer. You want this, you do that prayer. In fact in Hindus, we have a weekly timetable. Monday, you go to this temple. Tuesday, you go there. Wednesday, you go here. Thursday, one more song. Friday, some other ritual. In the weekly list, I don't think Krishna is there. As of now, it's not there. And in some seasons, they replace the list with the seasonal rituals will be there. They replace with that. So now, this table devotion, that is everybody knows. It's called table devotion. Vidura, Maitreya is not talking about table devotion. Or seasonal devotion. Suddenly you become big devotee. And after that, devotion is not visible. No. So Ekabhakti means Nityam. Nityam Bhagavata Sevaya. Continuously, our devotion to Krishna is unceasing. Whether you have trouble, no trouble, Lord gives, whether you are up or down, victory, failure, or rich, this, that, anything, our devotion to Krishna should not stop. This is called Ekabhakti Govinda. If you only do Bhakti when we are in suffering, and when we get something it's all in the cloud. Nowadays everything is in the cloud, right? Our Bhakti is also in the cloud. As soon as we get something, cloud. So that is the issue. So as long as this Bhakti cloud is there, then our questions will continue. So here it is said, Jatayikha Bhaktir Govinde Tebhyas Cho Upararama Vidura retired from his questioning as soon as he realized that exclusive devotion to Krishna is the goal of life.

---

Now how do I practice this? Exclusive? Actually once I was talking to one Muslim woman and I was talking about this. Exclusive Bhakti to God. Remove all our anxieties and miseries and all those things. But here Bhagavatam is saying you go to the God, you develop love for God. Because to develop our attraction towards the Lord. We pray to God in order to increase our attraction to Krishna. We pray to God Krishna in order to increase our service to the Lord. Nowhere a devotee will ask Krishna I am having this trouble, you please remove this. He will not ask. Haridasa Thakur was beaten up in 21 places by a whip. He never once asked my Lord you come and you know, relieve me from this suffering. No. Prahalas was put into so much of difficulty. His own father Heranakashyapu has put Prahalad, Bhakt
